chapters.title: Australia and its People
chapters.subtitle: Geography, symbols & identity
chapters.description: Learn about Australia's geography, national symbols, its diverse people, and what makes Australia unique as a nation.
key_topics: Australia is a country, continent and island

sections.title: Australia's Place in the World
sections.content:
Australia is a unique nation — it is simultaneously a country, a continent, and the world's largest island. Located in the southern hemisphere, Australia is bordered by the Indian Ocean to the west and the Pacific Ocean to the east. Australia's close neighbours include Indonesia to the north and New Zealand to the southeast.

Australia is the sixth-largest country in the world by area, covering approximately 7.7 million square kilometres. Despite its massive size, Australia has a relatively small population of about 26 million people.

sections.title: States and Territories
sections.content:
Australia has six states: New South Wales (NSW), Victoria (VIC), Queensland (QLD), South Australia (SA), Western Australia (WA), and Tasmania (TAS).

Australia also has two self-governing territories: the Australian Capital Territory (ACT), which contains the national capital Canberra, and the Northern Territory (NT).

Each state has its own capital city:
• New South Wales — Sydney
• Victoria — Melbourne
• Queensland — Brisbane
• South Australia — Adelaide
• Western Australia — Perth
• Tasmania — Hobart

Canberra is Australia's national capital, purpose-built as a compromise between rival cities Sydney and Melbourne.

sections.title: National Symbols
sections.content:
Australia's national flag features three elements:
• The Union Jack — representing Australia's historical links with Britain
• The Commonwealth Star — with 7 points (6 for states, 1 for territories)
• The Southern Cross — representing Australia's location in the southern hemisphere

Australia's national colours are green and gold. The national flower is the Golden Wattle. The national animal emblems are the kangaroo and emu, both appearing on the Commonwealth Coat of Arms. Neither animal can easily move backwards — symbolising a nation always moving forward.

Australia's national anthem is Advance Australia Fair, adopted in 1984.

sections.title: Australia's People
sections.content:
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples are Australia's original inhabitants, with a history and culture spanning at least 60,000 years — one of the oldest living cultures on Earth. There are hundreds of different Aboriginal nations and language groups.

European settlement began in 1788 with the arrival of the First Fleet. Since then, Australia has welcomed millions of migrants from all over the world, making it one of the most culturally diverse nations on Earth.

Today, Australia is a multicultural society. About one in four Australians were born overseas. Australia's people come from over 200 countries and speak hundreds of languages.

chapters.title: Democratic Beliefs
chapters.subtitle: Rights, freedoms & values
chapters.description: Understand Australia's democratic system, the freedoms it protects, and the values that make Australian society fair and free.

sections.title: Australia's Democratic System
sections.content:
Australia is a parliamentary democracy, based on the Westminster system inherited from Britain. This means Australian citizens elect their representatives to Parliament, and these representatives make decisions on behalf of the people.

Australia is also a constitutional monarchy — the King is the head of state, represented in Australia by the Governor-General. However, the day-to-day running of the country is carried out by the elected government and Parliament.

sections.title: Democratic Freedoms
sections.content:
Australia protects fundamental democratic freedoms for all people:

• Freedom of speech — the right to express opinions and views peacefully, within the law
• Freedom of religion — the right to practise any religion or no religion
• Freedom of association — the right to join (or not join) any legal organisation
• Freedom of movement — the right to move freely within Australia and travel overseas
• Freedom of the press — the media can report on government activities

These freedoms must be exercised within the law — for example, freedom of speech does not include inciting violence or hatred.

sections.title: The Rule of Law
sections.content:
One of Australia's most important democratic principles is the rule of law. This means:

• Everyone — including government officials, politicians, police, and judges — must follow the law
• No one is above the law
• All people are equal before the law
• Laws must be made through proper democratic processes
• People have the right to a fair trial

The rule of law protects everyone's rights and ensures that power cannot be abused.

sections.title: The Fair Go
sections.content:
The concept of 'a fair go' is central to Australian culture and values. It means that every person deserves an equal opportunity to succeed in life, regardless of their background, wealth, or where they came from.

This principle extends to all areas of life — education, employment, sport, and participation in community life. It means judging people on their efforts and abilities, not on who they are or where they come from.

The fair go underpins many of Australia's laws, including anti-discrimination laws that protect all people from unfair treatment.

chapters.title: Government & the Law
chapters.subtitle: How Australia is governed
chapters.description: Learn about Australia's three levels of government, how Parliament works, the role of the courts, and how laws are made.

sections.title: Three Levels of Government
sections.content:
Australia has three levels of government, each with different responsibilities:

1. Federal (national) government
   • Defence and national security
   • Immigration and border protection
   • Foreign affairs and trade
   • Social security and Medicare
   • Taxation policy

2. State and territory governments
   • Schools and education
   • Hospitals and health services
   • Police and courts
   • State roads and transport
   • Land management

3. Local government (councils)
   • Local roads and footpaths
   • Parks and recreation
   • Rubbish collection
   • Local planning and building approvals
   • Libraries and community services

sections.title: Federal Parliament
sections.content:
Australia's Federal Parliament consists of two houses:

The Senate (upper house)
• Represents the states and territories equally
• 12 senators per state, 2 per territory
• Senators serve 6-year terms
• Reviews legislation passed by the House

The House of Representatives (lower house)
• Represents the people based on population
• Members serve up to 3-year terms
• The government must have majority support here
• Where legislation usually starts

Both houses must pass a bill before it can become law. The Governor-General then gives Royal Assent.

sections.title: The Head of State and Government
sections.content:
Australia's head of state is the King (currently King Charles III). The King is represented in Australia by the Governor-General, who:
• Is appointed on the advice of the Prime Minister
• Opens and closes Parliament
• Signs bills into law
• Swears in ministers
• Plays an important constitutional role in times of crisis

The Prime Minister is the head of government — the leader of the party or coalition with majority support in the House of Representatives. The Prime Minister leads the Cabinet, which is the key decision-making body.

sections.title: The Australian Courts
sections.content:
Australia's court system interprets and applies the law:

The High Court of Australia is the highest court, and its decisions are final. It:
• Interprets the Australian Constitution
• Hears appeals from lower courts
• Has original jurisdiction over some matters

Other federal courts include the Federal Court and the Federal Circuit and Family Court.

Each state also has its own court hierarchy — Magistrates Courts, District/County Courts, and Supreme Courts.

The separation of powers ensures that courts are independent from the government — no politician can tell a judge how to decide a case.

sections.title: Elections and Voting
sections.content:
Voting in federal elections is compulsory for all enrolled Australian citizens aged 18 and over. Failing to vote without a valid reason can result in a fine.

Australia uses preferential (ranked choice) voting for the House of Representatives — voters rank candidates in order of preference.

The Senate uses proportional representation, which helps ensure a range of parties are represented.

Elections must be held at least every 3 years for the House of Representatives. The Prime Minister chooses the specific date within this timeframe.

chapters.title: Australian Values
chapters.subtitle: What we stand for
chapters.description: The values that define Australian society — equality, respect, freedom, and compassion. These are essential for the citizenship test.

sections.title: Core Australian Values
sections.content:
Australian values are the beliefs and principles that most Australians share and that define Australian society:

• Respect for the equal worth and dignity of all people
• Freedom and democracy
• The rule of law
• Tolerance and mutual respect
• A 'fair go' for everyone
• Mateship — friendship, loyalty, and helping others
• Care for the environment
• Compassion for those in need
• Commitment to the community

These values apply to all people in Australia, regardless of their cultural or religious background.

sections.title: Equality and Non-Discrimination
sections.content:
A fundamental Australian value is that all people are equal — men and women, people of all races, religions, and backgrounds.

This means:
• Men and women have equal rights in all areas of life
• Discrimination based on race, sex, disability, religion, or age is illegal
• People with disabilities are valued members of society with equal rights
• LGBTQ+ people are protected from discrimination
• People of all cultural backgrounds are treated with equal respect

Australia has strong anti-discrimination laws to protect these rights.

sections.title: Family, Relationships and Violence
sections.content:
Australian law strongly protects individuals within families and relationships:

• Marriage must be entered into freely by both partners — forced marriage is illegal
• Family violence (domestic violence) is completely illegal in Australia, regardless of cultural or religious background
• Child abuse and exploitation are serious crimes
• All marriages are between consenting adults
• Female genital mutilation is illegal

If you or someone you know is experiencing family violence, help is available 24/7 through 1800RESPECT (1800 737 732).

sections.title: Religion and Culture
sections.content:
Australia values religious freedom and cultural diversity:

• All Australians can practise any religion, or no religion, freely
• No religion is 'official' or government-sanctioned
• Religious beliefs are respected, but religion does not override Australian law
• Cultural diversity is celebrated — migrants are encouraged to maintain their heritage
• All cultural practices must comply with Australian law

This means that while religious and cultural practices are respected, they cannot justify discrimination, violence, or breaking the law.

chapters.title: Rights & Responsibilities
chapters.subtitle: Citizenship privileges and duties
chapters.description: Understand what Australian citizenship means — the rights it gives you and the responsibilities you take on.

sections.title: Rights of Australian Citizenship
sections.content:
Australian citizenship comes with important rights:

• Vote in federal, state, and local government elections
• Apply for an Australian passport
• Apply for jobs in the Australian Public Service (including security-sensitive roles)
• Seek election to parliament
• Ask for help from Australian consulates when overseas
• Apply for citizenship for children born overseas

These rights are in addition to the rights all people in Australia enjoy, such as equality before the law and freedom of religion.

sections.title: Responsibilities of Australian Citizenship
sections.content:
With rights come important responsibilities:

• Vote in federal and state elections (compulsory — you must enrol and vote)
• Obey Australian laws
• Be willing to serve on a jury if called
• If called upon, defend Australia
• Respect the rights and freedoms of others
• Support and uphold Australian democratic values

Enrolling on the electoral roll is also compulsory for citizens aged 18 and over.

sections.title: The Citizenship Pledge
sections.content:
When becoming an Australian citizen, you make a Pledge of Commitment:

'From this time forward, [under God,] I pledge my loyalty to Australia and its people, whose democratic beliefs I share, whose rights and liberties I respect, and whose laws I will uphold and obey.'

This pledge represents a personal commitment to Australia — to its people, its democratic values, and its laws. It is the beginning of your life as an Australian citizen.

sections.title: Permanent Residency vs Citizenship
sections.content:
Permanent residents have many of the same day-to-day rights as citizens, but not all:

Permanent residents can:
• Live and work in Australia permanently
• Access Medicare and some government services
• Sponsor some family members
• Travel to Australia on their visa

Only Australian citizens can:
• Vote in federal elections (and state elections in most states)
• Hold an Australian passport
• Apply for security-sensitive government jobs requiring citizenship
• Be elected to Parliament
• Receive automatic right of entry without a visa

chapters.title: History & Geography
chapters.subtitle: Australia's story and land
chapters.description: Explore Australia's rich history from ancient Aboriginal culture through European settlement, Federation, and becoming the modern nation it is today.

sections.title: Ancient Australia — First Australians
sections.content:
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have lived in Australia for at least 60,000 years, making them custodians of one of the world's oldest living cultures.

There were hundreds of distinct Aboriginal nations across Australia, each with their own language, culture, laws, and spiritual connection to their land. Aboriginal Australians had sophisticated knowledge of the land, water, and seasons.

Torres Strait Islander peoples lived on the islands between Australia and Papua New Guinea, developing distinct cultures closely connected to the sea.

sections.title: European Exploration and Settlement
sections.content:
European exploration of Australia began in the early 1600s:

• 1606 — Willem Janszoon (Dutch) made the first recorded European landing in Australia
• 1642 — Abel Tasman discovered Tasmania and New Zealand
• 1770 — Captain James Cook mapped the eastern coast, claiming it for Britain

British settlement began on January 26, 1788, when the First Fleet — 11 ships carrying 1,487 people (including about 750 convicts) — arrived at Sydney Cove under the command of Captain Arthur Phillip.

Phillip became Australia's first Governor and established the first European settlement at what is now Sydney.

sections.title: Growth and Change
sections.content:
Through the 1800s, Australia grew rapidly:

• More free settlers arrived alongside convicts
• Six separate colonies were established
• The Gold Rush of the 1850s (especially in Victoria) brought thousands of migrants from around the world, including significant numbers from China
• Aboriginal Australians were pushed off their lands and suffered greatly from diseases, violence, and loss of culture

In 1902, Australia became one of the first countries in the world to give women the right to vote at the national level (though Aboriginal women and men were still excluded in many places).

sections.title: Federation and World Wars
sections.content:
On January 1, 1901, the six Australian colonies united to form the Commonwealth of Australia — an historic act of Federation. Melbourne served as the temporary capital until Canberra was built.

Australia participated in both World Wars:

World War I (1914-1918): Australian and New Zealand troops (ANZACs) landed at Gallipoli, Turkey on April 25, 1915. Though the campaign failed militarily, the courage and sacrifice of ANZAC soldiers forged a powerful national identity. ANZAC Day is now Australia's most solemn national day of remembrance.

World War II (1939-1945): Australia faced direct threat from Japan, which bombed Darwin in 1942.

sections.title: Modern Australia
sections.content:
After World War II, Australia welcomed large numbers of migrants from Britain, Europe, and later Asia, transforming into a multicultural nation.

Key milestones in modern Australia:

• 1967 — Landmark referendum: 90%+ Australians voted 'Yes' to count Aboriginal Australians in the census and give the federal government power to make laws for them
• 1973 — White Australia Policy effectively ended; migration opened to all nationalities
• 1975 — Racial Discrimination Act passed
• 1992 — Mabo decision: High Court recognised Aboriginal native title, overturning 'terra nullius'
• 2000 — Sydney hosted the Olympic Games
• 2008 — Prime Minister Kevin Rudd delivered a formal apology to the Stolen Generations

Today Australia is a prosperous, diverse, and democratic nation, home to people from over 200 countries.
